ראש.bdb: head; as first in position, rank, or time; beginning
ראש.explanation:
The root *ראש (rōš)* means head in its most concrete sense — the physical head of a human or animal body, the top or uppermost part of any object. This spatial meaning extends to the head of a valley, the head of a river, the summit of a mountain, and the capital of a column. BDB defines it as "head; as first in position, rank, or time; beginning," capturing its tangible spatial reference points.

From this concrete starting point, *ראש (rōš)* extends abstractly into rank (chief, leader, ruler), time (beginning, first), quantity (sum, total, capital), and quality (best, choicest). This single Hebrew root replaces at least eight distinct English words: head, beginning, chief, first, top, summit, capital, and principal. Strong's H7218 documents its 598 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible, demonstrating its fundamental role in Hebrew thought.

ברא.bdb: to create — used exclusively of divine creation; never with a human subject in the qal stem
ברא.explanation:
The root *ברא (bārāʾ)* in its concrete sense refers to the divine creative act of bringing something into existence. BDB notes it is "used exclusively of divine creation; never with a human subject in the qal stem," marking it as a uniquely theological verb. The concrete act is one of absolute origination — God creating heavens, earth, animals, and humans.

Abstractly, *ברא (bārāʾ)* extends to concepts of innovation, formation of something qualitatively new, and the establishment of order from chaos. This single root replaces at least five English words: create, make, form, produce, and fashion. Strong's H1254 records 54 occurrences, concentrated in Genesis 1 and Isaiah's new creation prophecies, showing its specialized theological usage.

אלה.bdb: God — plural form אלהים is the standard Biblical Hebrew word for God; plural in form, singular in usage when referring to the God of Israel
אלה.explanation:
Concretely, *אלה (ʾĕlōah)* refers to a mighty one, a powerful being with supernatural strength. BDB defines it as "God — plural form אלהים is the standard Biblical Hebrew word for God; plural in form, singular in usage when referring to the God of Israel." The singular form appears most frequently in Job, emphasizing divine power and majesty.

Abstractly, this root extends to concepts of deity, divinity, object of worship, and ultimate authority. The plural form *אלהים (ʾĕlōhîm)* with singular verbs creates a theological tension between unity and plurality that runs through the entire Hebrew Bible. This root replaces at least four English words: God, deity, divine being, and mighty one. Strong's H433 documents 57 occurrences of the singular form.

ארץ.bdb: earth, land — the whole earth as opposed to heaven; also a specific territory or country
ארץ.explanation:
The root *ארץ (ʾereṣ)* in its concrete sense means ground, soil, terrain — the physical earth beneath one's feet. BDB defines it as "earth, land — the whole earth as opposed to heaven; also a specific territory or country." This includes cultivated land, wilderness, and the material substance of the planet.

Abstract extensions include territory (a specific country), the whole world (as opposed to heaven), and the realm of human habitation. The word appears approximately 2,500 times in the Hebrew Bible, making it one of the most common nouns. This single root replaces at least six English words: earth, land, ground, country, territory, and world. Strong's H776 captures its dual concrete/abstract range.

שמי.bdb: heavens, sky — dual form; the visible expanse above the earth and the dwelling place of God
שמי.explanation:
Concretely, *שמי (šāmayim)* refers to the sky, the visible heavens, the atmospheric expanse above the earth. BDB notes it is "heavens, sky — dual form; the visible expanse above the earth and the dwelling place of God." The dual form may reflect the ancient conception of waters above and below the firmament.

Abstractly, it extends to heaven as the divine abode, the celestial realm, and the spiritual domain where God dwells. The word exists only in plural form, with no singular equivalent in Hebrew. This root replaces at least five English words: heaven, sky, heavens, firmament, and celestial. Strong's H8064 documents 420 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

היה.gloss: to be, to exist, to become
היה.bdb: to be, become, come to pass, exist — the most fundamental Hebrew verb of being; root of the divine name YHWH
היה.explanation:
The root *היה (hāyāh)* concretely means to exist, to be present, to have being. BDB defines it as "to be, become, come to pass, exist — the most fundamental Hebrew verb of being; root of the divine name YHWH." It describes states of existence and presence in the physical world.

Abstract extensions include becoming (process of change), happening (events coming to pass), and the philosophical concept of being itself. This is the verbal root of the divine name *יהוה (YHWH)* — "he who is" or "he who causes to be." The root replaces at least six English words: be, exist, become, happen, occur, and come to pass. Strong's H1961 records 3,560 occurrences, making it one of the most frequent verbs.

חשך.bdb: darkness — the absence of light; used both literally and figuratively for moral and spiritual darkness
חשך.explanation:
Concretely, *חשך (ḥōšeḵ)* means darkness, the absence of light, physical obscurity. BDB defines it as "darkness — the absence of light; used both literally and figuratively for moral and spiritual darkness." This includes night darkness, eclipse, and any situation where light is absent.

Abstract extensions include ignorance (lack of knowledge), calamity (dark times), evil (moral darkness), and divine judgment (hiding of God's face). The contrast with *אור (ʾôr)* (light) structures much of Hebrew poetic and prophetic literature. This root replaces at least five English words: darkness, obscurity, gloom, ignorance, and calamity. Strong's H2822 documents 80 occurrences.

רוח.bdb: breath, wind, spirit — the animating life-force; simultaneously wind, breath, and spirit in Hebrew thought; cannot be reduced to one meaning
רוח.explanation:
The root *רוח (rûaḥ)* concretely means wind, breath, air in motion. BDB captures its semantic range as "breath, wind, spirit — the animating life-force; simultaneously wind, breath, and spirit in Hebrew thought; cannot be reduced to one meaning." This includes atmospheric wind, human breath, and the breath of life in creatures.

Abstractly, it extends to spirit (divine or human), attitude, disposition, mind, and the non-material aspect of being. The word's inability to be pinned to a single English equivalent reflects a holistic Hebrew worldview where physical and spiritual are not rigidly separated. This root replaces at least six English words: spirit, wind, breath, air, mind, and attitude. Strong's H7307 records 378 occurrences.

רחף.bdb: to hover, to flutter — as a bird over its nest; appears only twice in the entire Hebrew Bible: here and in Deuteronomy 32:11
רחף.explanation:
Concretely, *רחף (rāḥap̄)* means to hover, to flutter, as a bird over its nest. BDB defines it as "to hover, to flutter — as a bird over its nest; appears only twice in the entire Hebrew Bible: here and in Deuteronomy 32:11." The concrete image is maternal and protective.

Abstractly, it extends to divine presence hovering protectively, careful oversight, and nurturing attention. The rarity of the word (only two occurrences) makes each instance particularly significant. This root replaces at least four English words: hover, flutter, brood over, and move gently. Strong's H7363 marks its exceptional status in the biblical lexicon.

הום.bdb: to make a loud sound, to roar, to surge — used of rushing water and tumult; root of תהום, the primeval deep
הום.explanation:
The root *הום (hûm)* concretely means to roar, to surge, to make a loud sound. BDB defines it as "to make a loud sound, to roar, to surge — used of rushing water and tumult; root of תהום, the primeval deep." This includes the sound of rushing waters, crowd noise, and chaotic commotion.

Abstract extensions include tumult, confusion, disorder, and the chaotic state preceding creation. As the root of *תהום (tĕhôm)* (the deep), it connects sound with the primordial waters of Genesis 1:2. This root replaces at least four English words: roar, surge, make noise, and be tumultuous. Strong's H1949 documents its limited but significant usage.

פנה.bdb: face, presence — פנים always appears in plural form; construct פני means face of or presence of
פנה.explanation:
Concretely, *פנה (pānāh)* means face, the front part of the head or any surface. BDB notes "face, presence — פנים always appears in plural form; construct פני means face of or presence of." The plural form may originally have captured multiple facial features.

Abstractly, it extends to presence (being before someone), attention (turning one's face toward), favor (shining face), and orientation (direction faced). The "face of God" becomes a central theological concept representing divine presence and attention. This root replaces at least five English words: face, presence, surface, turn, and attention. Strong's H6437 records 2,109 occurrences.

אמר.bdb: to say, to speak, to command — the most common Hebrew verb for speech; implies intentional declaration
אמר.explanation:
The root *אמר (ʾāmar)* concretely means to say, to speak, to utter words. BDB defines it as "to say, to speak, to command — the most common Hebrew verb for speech; implies intentional declaration." This includes all forms of verbal communication.

Abstract extensions include thinking (saying in one's heart), commanding (authoritative speech), and declaring (formal proclamation). With over 5,300 occurrences, it is the most common verb in the Hebrew Bible. This root replaces at least five English words: say, speak, tell, command, and declare. Strong's H559 demonstrates its foundational role in Hebrew narrative and discourse.

אור.bdb: light — natural and divine illumination; the first thing spoken into existence in Genesis 1
אור.explanation:
Concretely, *אור (ʾôr)* means light, physical illumination, brightness. BDB defines it as "light — natural and divine illumination; the first thing spoken into existence in Genesis 1." This includes sunlight, moonlight, lamplight, and any source of visible radiation.

Abstractly, it extends to enlightenment (understanding), joy (light of countenance), salvation (light of deliverance), and divine presence (God is my light). The creation of light in Genesis 1:3 establishes it as the fundamental element of ordered reality. This root replaces at least four English words: light, illumination, brightness, and enlightenment. Strong's H216 documents 120 occurrences.

ראה.bdb: to see, to look, to perceive — the same ordinary verb for human and divine sight; no elevated vocabulary for God seeing
ראה.explanation:
The root *ראה (rāʾāh)* concretely means to see, to look, visual perception with the eyes. BDB notes "to see, to look, to perceive — the same ordinary verb for human and divine sight; no elevated vocabulary for God seeing." This includes all forms of visual observation.

Abstract extensions include understanding (seeing with the mind), experiencing (seeing trouble), providing (God will see to it), and visiting (seeing after someone). The same verb describes both human and divine perception without distinction. This root replaces at least five English words: see, look, behold, perceive, and understand. Strong's H7200 records 1,309 occurrences.

טוב.bdb: good, pleasant, beautiful, right — moral, aesthetic, and functional goodness; appears 7 times in Genesis 1
טוב.explanation:
Concretely, *טוב (ṭôḇ)* means good, pleasant, agreeable to the senses. BDB defines it as "good, pleasant, beautiful, right — moral, aesthetic, and functional goodness; appears 7 times in Genesis 1." This includes good food, pleasant appearance, and agreeable circumstances.

Abstractly, it extends to moral goodness (ethical rightness), beauty (aesthetic excellence), benefit (what is advantageous), and righteousness (conformity to divine standard). Its sevenfold repetition in Genesis 1 connects it with the completeness of creation. This root replaces at least five English words: good, pleasant, beautiful, beneficial, and right. Strong's H2896 documents 739 occurrences.

בדל.bdb: to separate, to divide — used of physical separation and of ritual distinction between holy and common, clean and unclean
בדל.explanation:
The root *בדל (bādal)* concretely means to separate, to divide, to put space between physical objects. BDB defines it as "to separate, to divide — used of physical separation and of ritual distinction between holy and common, clean and unclean." This includes dividing light from darkness, waters from waters.

Abstract extensions include distinguishing (making conceptual distinctions), setting apart (for holy use), and the fundamental act of creating order through categorization. The Levitical holiness code builds on this primeval act of separation. This root replaces at least four English words: separate, divide, distinguish, and set apart. Strong's H914 records 42 occurrences.

בין.bdb: to understand, to discern; as preposition: between, among — the same root carries both perceiving and separating
בין.explanation:
Concretely, *בין (bîn)* means to discern, to perceive difference, to distinguish between things. BDB notes "to understand, to discern; as preposition: between, among — the same root carries both perceiving and separating." This includes visual and intellectual discrimination.

Abstractly, it extends to understanding (comprehension), insight (deep perception), wisdom (applied understanding), and the spatial concept "between" (the gap that enables distinction). The etymological connection between perceiving and the space between is uniquely Hebrew. This root replaces at least four English words: understand, discern, perceive, and have insight. Strong's H995 documents 169 occurrences.

קרא.gloss: to call, to name, to proclaim
קרא.bdb: to call, to name, to read aloud — naming in Hebrew implies authority over the named; to name something is to define its essential nature and establish one's lordship over it
קרא.explanation:
The root *קרא (qārāʾ)* concretely means to call, to call out, to name, to read aloud, to summon. BDB defines it as "to call by name — naming in Hebrew implies authority over the named; to name something is to define its essential nature and establish one's lordship over it." This includes divine naming (as in Genesis 1:5), human calling, and public proclamation.

Abstractly, it extends to concepts of designation (assigning identity), invitation (calling to come), proclamation (announcing publicly), and reading (calling out written words). The act of naming in Genesis 1:5 completes the creative separation of light and darkness by giving them ordered identities within the created rhythm. This root replaces at least six English words: call, name, proclaim, read, summon, and invite. Strong's H7121 documents 735 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

אחד.bdb: one — the cardinal number; also used to express unity or uniqueness
אחד.explanation:
The root *אחד (ʾeḥāḏ)* concretely means one, the cardinal number representing singularity. BDB defines it as "one — the cardinal number; also used to express unity or uniqueness." This includes numerical oneness, unified groups, and unique entities.

Abstractly, it extends to concepts of unity (bringing together disparate elements), uniqueness (being the only one of its kind), and singularity (indivisible wholeness). In Genesis 1:5, the phrase *יום אחד (yôm ʾeḥāḏ)* (day one) uses the cardinal number rather than the ordinal *ראשון (riʾšôn)* (first), creating a distinctive pattern where only the first day is numbered this way. This root replaces at least three English words: one, unity, and unique. Strong's H259 documents over 950 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

תהו.bdb: formlessness, confusion, unreality, emptiness — that which is unreal or illusory
תהו.explanation:
The root *תהו (ṯōhû)* concretely means formlessness, chaos, emptiness. BDB defines it as "formlessness, confusion, unreality, emptiness — that which is unreal or illusory." This includes the pre-creation state described in Genesis 1:2, devastated landscapes, and unreal illusions.

Abstractly, it extends to concepts of chaos (absence of order), futility (meaningless effort), and desolation (complete emptiness). The word appears 20 times in the Hebrew Bible, often paired with *בהו (bōhû)* as *תהו ובהו (ṯōhû wāḇōhû)* — a rhyming phrase that captures the sound and feel of emptiness. This root replaces at least four English words: formlessness, chaos, emptiness, and desolation. Strong's H8414 marks its significant role in describing pre-creation and post-judgment states.

בהו.bdb: emptiness, void — appears only 3 times in the entire Hebrew Bible, always alongside תהו
בהו.explanation:
The root *בהו (bōhû)* concretely means void, emptiness. BDB defines it as "emptiness, void — appears only 3 times in the entire Hebrew Bible, always alongside תהו." This includes the complete absence of substance or form, the empty space that accompanies chaos.

Abstractly, it extends to concepts of nothingness (total absence), vacancy (unfilled space), and desolation (barren emptiness). Its exclusive pairing with *תהו (ṯōhû)* suggests it may have been a fixed poetic pair from ancient oral tradition. The rhyming quality of *תהו ובהו (ṯōhû wāḇōhû)* creates an auditory experience of emptiness that transcends translation. This root replaces at least three English words: void, emptiness, and nothingness. Strong's H922 highlights its rarity and specialized usage.

מים.bdb: waters — exists only in plural form in Hebrew; water is inherently conceived as plural
מים.explanation:
The root *מים (mayim)* concretely means waters, the liquid substance essential for life. BDB defines it as "waters — exists only in plural form in Hebrew; water is inherently conceived as plural." This includes rivers, seas, floods, springs, rain, and all forms of liquid water.

Abstractly, it extends to concepts of life-source (water as sustainer), chaos (the primordial waters), purification (ritual washing), and refreshment (spiritual renewal). Like *שמים (šāmayim)* (heavens), *מים (mayim)* has no singular form in Biblical Hebrew, reflecting a holistic conception where water is inherently multiple and collective. This root replaces at least three English words: waters, water, and liquid. Strong's H4325 documents approximately 580 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

יום.bdb: day — period of daylight, 24-hour period, or indefinite period of time
יום.explanation:
The root *יום (yôm)* concretely means day, the period of daylight or a 24-hour cycle. BDB defines it as "day — period of daylight, 24-hour period, or indefinite period of time." This includes the daylight hours, complete day-night cycles, and extended periods of time.

Abstractly, it extends to concepts of time (measured duration), era (significant period), and opportunity (day of salvation). With over 2,300 occurrences, it is one of the most common nouns in the Hebrew Bible. The ambiguity of *יום (yôm)* — whether literal 24-hour days or longer creative periods — has been central to interpretation of Genesis 1. This root replaces at least four English words: day, time, period, and era. Strong's H3117 demonstrates its fundamental role in Hebrew temporal thought.

לילה.bdb: night — the dark period between evening and morning; used both literally and figuratively
לילה.explanation:
The root *לילה (lāyĕlāh)* concretely means night, the period of darkness between evening and morning. BDB defines it as "night — the dark period between evening and morning; used both literally and figuratively for times of calamity, moral darkness, or divine hiddenness." This includes physical nighttime, periods of darkness, and metaphorical nights of trouble.

Abstractly, it extends to concepts of ignorance (lack of illumination), danger (time of vulnerability), and divine absence (when God seems hidden). The word appears about 230 times in the Hebrew Bible. In Genesis 1:5, *לילה (lāyĕlāh)* is the name given to darkness, transforming chaotic *חשך (ḥōšeḵ)* into an ordered, named part of creation's rhythm. This root replaces at least three English words: night, darkness, and nighttime. Strong's H3915 captures its dual literal/figurative usage.

ערב.bdb: evening, dusk — the transition period when daylight fades into night
ערב.explanation:
The root *ערב (ʿereḇ)* concretely means evening, dusk, the transition from day to night. BDB defines it as "evening — the period between afternoon and night; the time of the evening sacrifice in the Temple." This includes the fading light of day, the onset of darkness, and the time of day's end.

Abstractly, it extends to concepts of completion (day's work finished), mixture (as the root may relate to mixing or becoming dark), and the beginning of sacred time (since the Hebrew day begins at evening). In Genesis 1:5 and throughout the creation account, *ערב (ʿereḇ)* precedes *בקר (bōqer)* (morning), establishing the pattern "evening and morning" that defines the Hebrew day. This root replaces at least three English words: evening, dusk, and nightfall. Strong's H6153 documents approximately 130 occurrences.

בקר.bdb: morning, dawn — the breaking of light after darkness; connected to examining or seeking
בקר.explanation:
The root *בקר (bōqer)* concretely means morning, dawn, the breaking of light after darkness. BDB defines it as "morning, dawn — the breaking of light after darkness; connected to examining or seeking (as a shepherd examines his flock in morning light)." This includes sunrise, early daylight, and the start of the day's activities.

Abstractly, it extends to concepts of new beginning (fresh start after night), examination (careful inspection in good light), and hope (light after darkness). In Genesis 1:5 and throughout the creation narrative, *בקר (bōqer)* follows *ערב (ʿereḇ)* (evening), completing the daily cycle. The verbal root may relate to seeking or investigating, connecting morning light with clarity and discernment. This root replaces at least three English words: morning, dawn, and daybreak. Strong's H1242 documents approximately 210 occurrences.

רקע.bdb: to beat out, stamp, spread — as metal hammered flat; then the solid or extended vault of heaven
רקע.explanation:
The root *רקע (rāqaʿ)* means to beat out, stamp, or spread flat, as a metalsmith hammers metal thin. BDB defines it as to stamp, beat out flat — used of spreading metal, and of God spreading out the earth and sky. The derived noun *רקיע (rāqîaʿ)* — expanse, firmament — is used 17 times in the Hebrew Bible, concentrated in Genesis 1 (9 times) and Ezekiel 1 and 10.

The word entered English through the LXX Greek *stereōma* (firm, solid thing), then Latin *firmamentum*, then English *firmament*. Modern translations prefer *expanse* (ESV, NIV) or *vault* (NRSV), reflecting ongoing debate about whether the ancient conception imagined a solid dome or a stretched-out space. Genesis 1 uses *rāqîaʿ* as the structure that separates upper waters from lower waters and serves as the platform for the luminaries. Ezekiel's visions describe it as an awe-inspiring crystal surface above which God's throne is located. Strong's H7549.

קוה.bdb: to collect, bind together, gather; to wait with expectation, to hope
קוה.explanation:
The root *קוה (qāwāh)* carries two distinct but etymologically related ranges: (1) to gather, collect, bind — giving the noun *מקוה (miqweh)* (a gathered body of water, pool); and (2) to wait, expect, hope — giving *תקוה (tiqwāh)* (hope, expectation, cord). BDB documents both ranges and notes their likely connection through the image of strands gathered or stretched in expectation.

In Genesis 1:9, the Niphal form *יקוו (yiqqāwū)* — let them be gathered — uses the first range: the primordial waters are commanded to collect into defined places. In Isaiah 40:31, the same root appears in a famous hope passage: *those who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength (qōwê yhwh)* — using the second range. The word *Tikvah* (תקוה), meaning hope, is the modern Hebrew word for hope and the title of Israel's national anthem. Creation-vocabulary and hope-vocabulary share the same root. Strong's H6960.

יבש.bdb: to be dry, dried up, withered — of plants, rivers, land
יבש.explanation:
The root *יבש (yāḇaš)* means to be dry, to dry up, to wither — used of rivers drying up, plants withering, and land becoming arid. BDB documents the verbal root and its derivatives: the adjective *יבש (yāḇēš)* (dry, dried up) and the noun *יבשה (yabbāšāh)* (dry land, dry ground). Strong's H3004.

In Genesis 1:9–10, *יבשה (yabbāšāh)* — dry land — appears as the land that emerges when waters are gathered. The same root describes parched land in Jeremiah, drought conditions in Amos, and Ezekiel's valley of dry bones (*עצמות יבשות*, dry bones). The concept of dryness — land free of covering water — is the defining feature of habitable earth in the creation account. *יבשה* is later renamed *ארץ* (earth) in verse 10, but continues to appear as the technical term for the specific physical property of dry ground throughout the Hebrew Bible.

דשא.gloss: to sprout vegetation, bring forth green growth
דשא.bdb: to sprout, put forth green growth — said of the ground producing fresh vegetation
דשא.explanation:
The root *דשא (dāšāʾ)* is rare, appearing only twice in the Hebrew Bible: Genesis 1:11 (*תדשא הארץ*, let the earth sprout) and Joel 2:22 (*תדשא השדה*, the field will sprout). BDB defines it as to sprout, shoot forth — used specifically of ground putting forth fresh green growth. The derived noun *דשא (dešeʾ)* means new grass, fresh green vegetation, tender sprouts. Strong's H1876 and H1877.

The rarity of the verb *דשא* makes each appearance stand out. In Joel 2:22, it appears in a restoration promise — the scorched, locust-devoured land will sprout again. Genesis 1:11 uses it for the primordial act of earth bringing forth vegetation. The imagery in both cases is of sudden, vigorous green emergence from ground that was barren — in Genesis 1, because the ground had been under water; in Joel, because it had been devastated. *דשא* captures the drama of green life appearing from apparently lifeless ground.

זרע.bdb: to sow seed; noun: seed, sowing, offspring, descendants
זרע.explanation:
The root *זרע (zāraʿ)* covers both the agricultural act of sowing (to scatter seed on ground) and the result of that sowing (seed, offspring, descendants). BDB documents: verb *זרע (zāraʿ)* — to sow; noun *זרע (zeraʿ)* — seed, sowing, offspring. Strong's H2232 (verb) and H2233 (noun).

In Genesis 1:11–12, *זרע (zeraʿ)* refers to literal botanical seed — the reproductive units enclosed within fruit. The Hiphil participle *מזריע (mazrîaʿ)* (seed-bearing, seed-producing) describes seed-bearing plants. But the same noun's range — seed, offspring, descendants — makes it a crucial term in covenant theology: God promises Abraham *זרעך (zarʿĕḵā)* — your seed — meaning both his literal biological offspring and the entire covenant community. The seed of plants in Genesis 1 is the same word that carries all the weight of Abrahamic, Davidic, and messianic promise throughout the Hebrew Bible.

פרה.bdb: to bear fruit, to be fruitful — of plants, animals, and people
פרה.explanation:
The root *פרה (pārāh)* means to bear fruit, to be fruitful, to reproduce abundantly. BDB documents the verb and its derivatives: noun *פרי (pĕrî)* (fruit, fruitfulness, offspring). Strong's H6509 (verb) and H6529 (noun).

The botanical and biological ranges of this root are both present in Genesis 1. Fruit trees bear *פרי (pĕrî)* (fruit, v.11–12) and creatures and humans are commanded *פרו (pĕrū)* (be fruitful, v.22, 28). The same root underlies both the agricultural productivity of trees and the reproductive mandate given to living beings. This verbal-nominal connection is likely intentional: humans are to be fruitful as trees are fruitful — their productivity is a kind of God-given flowering. The fruitfulness mandate of Genesis 1:28 becomes one of the most frequently repeated divine promises in the Torah, appearing in renewed and expanded forms in Genesis 9, 17, 28, and 35.

עשה.bdb: to do, make, produce, accomplish, work — the most general verb for human and divine creative activity
עשה.explanation:
The root *עשה (ʿāśāh)* is one of the most common verbs in the Hebrew Bible, appearing over 2,600 times. BDB defines it comprehensively as to do, to make, to produce, to perform, to accomplish — covering the full range of activity from routine labor to divine creative work. Strong's H6213.

In Genesis 1, *עשה* appears in contrast to *ברא (bārāʾ)* (to create, uniquely divine). God *makes* (עשה) the expanse (v.7), the luminaries (v.16), and the land animals (v.25) — but *creates* (ברא) the world-fabric (v.1), animate life (v.21), and humanity (v.27). The distribution is theologically significant: *ברא* marks qualitatively new categories of reality; *עשה* marks the fashioning and ordering of materials. Both verbs are applied to God, but only *עשה* is applied to human craftsmen, builders, and workers throughout the rest of the Bible. God and humans share the vocabulary of making.

מין.bdb: kind, species — a category of organism that reproduces after its own kind
מין.explanation:
The noun *מין (mîn)* means kind, species — a category within the biological order. BDB defines it as kind, species — a group of organisms that share characteristic traits and reproduce within their group. Strong's H4327 documents 31 occurrences, 10 of which appear in Genesis 1 alone, making this chapter the primary site for the concept.

The phrase *למינו / למינה / למינהו / למינהם (lĕmînô/āh/êhū/êhem)* — according to its/their kind — appears 7 times in Genesis 1, functioning as a closing formula for each creative category of plant and animal. The repetition establishes kind-faithfulness — the principle that each biological category reproduces within itself — as the foundational law of created life. The Hebrew concept of *mîn* is not identical to the modern biological species but overlaps significantly. Young-earth creationist interpretation tends to identify *mîn* with a broader family-level category; evolutionary creationist interpretation sees it as describing the general principle of biological continuity. Strong's H4327.

שרץ.bdb: to swarm, to teem — used of the abundant, rapid movement of small creatures in water and on land
שרץ.explanation:
The root *שרץ (šāraṣ)* means to swarm, to teem — describing the frantic, abundant movement of masses of small creatures. BDB documents the verb and the derived noun *שרץ (šereṣ)* — swarm, swarming creatures. Strong's H8317 (verb) and H8318 (noun).

In Genesis 1:20–21, both verb (*ישרצו*, let them swarm) and noun (*שרץ*, swarms) appear together as a cognate accusative: the waters are to *swarm with swarming things*. This figure of speech — using verb and object from the same root — intensifies the meaning and creates memorable sound. The same root appears in Genesis 8:17 (the animals *swarm* out of the ark) and Exodus 7:28 (Egypt *swarms* with frogs). *שרץ* (swarming things) is also a category in Leviticus 11's food laws, identifying small crawling and swarming creatures as typically unclean. The Leviticus categories of clean and unclean explicitly reference the creation categories of Genesis 1.

נפש.bdb: soul, living being, life, self, desire, appetite, emotion — the animating inner life of a person or creature
נפש.explanation:
The noun *נפש (nep̄eš)* is one of the most semantically rich and theologically significant words in the Hebrew Bible. BDB traces its range from the concrete (throat, neck, the physical breath-canal) through vital breath and the animating life-force to the entire person viewed as a living, desiring, emotional self. Strong's H5315 documents approximately 754 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:20–21 and 24, *נפש חיה (nep̄eš ḥayyāh)* — living soul/creature — describes water creatures, land creatures, and birds. The same phrase appears in Genesis 2:7 when God breathes into the nostrils of the dust-formed human: *ויהי האדם לנפש חיה (wayhî hāʾādām lĕnep̄eš ḥayyāh)* — the man became a living soul. This makes *nep̄eš ḥayyāh* a category shared by animals and humans — both are living souls. *Nep̄eš* does not denote an immaterial component that survives death (as in the Greek *psychē*/*pneuma* distinction) but the whole self as experiencing, breathing, desiring creature. The concept is holistic, not dualistic.

עוף.bdb: to fly, to soar; noun: bird, flying creature
עוף.explanation:
The root *עוף (ʿûp̄)* means to fly, to soar. BDB documents the verb (to fly) and the derived noun *עוף (ʿôp̄)* — bird, flying creature; all things that fly. Strong's H5774 (verb) and H5775 (noun).

In Genesis 1:20–21, both the noun *עוף (ʿôp̄)* (birds) and the Piel intensive verb form *יעופף (yĕʿôp̄êp̄)* (to fly rapidly) appear: *ועוף יעופף (wĕʿôp̄ yĕʿôp̄êp̄)* — and birds will fly. This is another cognate construction, verb from root paired with noun from same root, creating an aurally vivid effect. The Piel *יעופף* doubles the root's central consonants, mimicking sound and motion. *עוף (ʿôp̄)* as a collective noun covers the entire category of flying creatures — birds, bats, and insects. In Leviticus 11:13–19, specific flying creatures are listed within this category as the bird-food-law section of the purity code.

רמש.gloss: to creep, crawl, move along the ground
רמש.bdb: to creep, to move — of small ground-level creatures; crawling, slithering locomotion
רמש.explanation:
The root *רמש (rāmaś)* means to creep, crawl, or move along the ground — describing the characteristic locomotion of small, low-moving creatures. BDB documents the verb (to creep) and the derived noun *רמש (remeś)* — creeping things, things that move at ground level. Strong's H7430 (verb) and H7431 (noun).

In Genesis 1, *רמש* and its noun *רמש (remeś)* appear in verses 21, 24, 25, 26, and 28 — five times — consistently designating the lowest category of land creatures. The word covers reptiles, insects, and other small, ground-level animals. In the dominion mandate of verse 28, humanity's dominion extends over *כל חיה הרמשת על הארץ (kol ḥayyāh hārōmeśeṯ ʿal hāʾāreṣ)* — every living thing that moves on the earth — using the Qal feminine participle. The same root appears in Leviticus 11:44 and Deuteronomy 14:19, where *שרץ (šereṣ)* and *רמש (remeś)* together mark small, typically unclean creeping animals.

ברך.bdb: to bless — to confer vitality, prosperity, and reproductive capacity; to praise; to kneel before
ברך.explanation:
The root *ברך (bāraḵ)* means to bless — in the Piel (the most common stem for this root), to confer a blessing, to bestow divine favor. BDB documents both the active (to bless a person or creature) and the passive/reflexive (to be blessed) senses. The related noun *ברכה (bĕrāḵāh)* means blessing. Strong's H1288 (verb) and H1293 (noun).

In Genesis 1:22, *ויברך אתם אלהים (wayyĕḇāreḵ ʾōṯām ʾĕlōhîm)* — and God blessed them — is the first occurrence of blessing in the Bible. The blessing confers reproductive capacity: be fruitful and multiply. Blessing in Genesis 1 is not affective (feeling good about someone) but performative (granting the capacity for life and increase). The same blessing formula recurs in verse 28 for humans. *ברך* is also the word for kneeling — bending the knee — and BDB notes a probable etymological link: to bless originally involved physical kneeling or prostration (BDB s.v. ברך). The root appears over 300 times in the Piel, making it one of the most frequent denominators of divine-human relationship in the entire Hebrew Bible.

מלא.bdb: to be full, to fill, to complete — of space, time, or quantity
מלא.explanation:
The root *מלא (māleʾ)* means to fill, to be or become full, to complete. BDB documents the Qal (to be full), Piel (to fill, to cause to be full), and other stems. Strong's H4390 documents approximately 252 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:22 and 28, the Qal imperative *מלאו (milʾū)* — fill! — commands creatures and humans to fill the domains of creation with their offspring. This command connects to the chapter's structural theme: the first three days establish the domains (empty); the last three days fill them with inhabitants. The *milʾū* command makes filling explicitly intentional: creatures are assigned to complete the filling that creation's architecture anticipated. The same root appears in Genesis 6:11 (*ותמלא הארץ חמס*, the earth was filled with violence) and Numbers 14:21 (*כל הארץ יכבוד יהוה*, the earth shall be filled with the glory of the LORD) — the earth can be filled with either corruption or glory.

נתן.bdb: to give, to put, to set, to appoint, to allow — the most versatile Hebrew verb for the act of granting or placing
נתן.explanation:
The root *נתן (nāṯan)* is the most common Hebrew verb for giving and granting, appearing approximately 2,000 times in the Hebrew Bible. BDB: to give, to put, to set, to appoint. The verb covers: giving a gift (v.29: I have given you...), placing something in a location (v.17: God placed them in the expanse), appointing someone to an office, and permitting. Strong's H5414.

In Genesis 1, *נתן* appears in two uses: the explicit grant of food to humans and animals (v.29: *נתתי לכם*, I have given you), and the placing of luminaries (v.17: *ויתן אתם*, he placed them). These two uses cover the full range: granting (giving food) and locating (placing luminaries). The same verb will become central to covenant language — God *gives* the land to Abraham (*ונתתי לך*, I will give to you; Gen 12:7, 15:18, etc.), the law to Moses, and the covenant to Israel. The word of creation (I have given you food) anticipates the word of covenant (I will give you the land).

כבש.bdb: to subdue, to bring into subjection, to tread down — used of military conquest, subjugating enemies, and cultivating land by effort
כבש.explanation:
The root *כבש (kāḇaš)* means to subdue, to bring under control, to tread down. BDB: to subdue, to bring into subjection — used of military subjugation of enemies and of forceful labor on the ground. Strong's H3533 documents approximately 15 occurrences.

*כבש* in Genesis 1:28 — *וכבשה (wĕḵiḇšuhā)* — subdue it (the earth) — is the strongest creative-mandate verb given to humans, stronger than *רדה (rādāh)* (rule, have dominion). It implies active, effortful work: the earth does not yield its goods automatically. Human vocation requires labor. This single word pre-dates and anticipates the labor elements of Genesis 3 (thorns, sweat, toil) — but where Genesis 3 makes labor painful, Genesis 1 makes it vocationally intentional. The dominion over creation is not exploitation; it is active stewardship requiring genuine human effort and care.

רדה.bdb: to rule, to have dominion, to govern — used of kings, rulers, and assigned governance over others
רדה.explanation:
The root *רדה (rādāh)* means to rule, to have dominion, to exercise governance. BDB: to rule, to govern — used of kings' governance (1 Kings 5:4) and of the psalmist's vision of the Davidic king's dominion (Ps 72:8). Strong's H7287 documents approximately 25 occurrences.

In Genesis 1:26 and 28, *רדה* describes humanity's divinely-assigned governance over the animal world: *וירדו (wĕyirdū)* — let them rule (v.26) and *ורדו (ûrĕḏū)* — and rule (v.28). The governance is explicitly delegated: humans rule as God's image-bearers, not as self-appointed owners. The mandate echoes the royal *rādāh* of Psalm 72:8 (the Davidic king's dominion from sea to sea), suggesting that human dominion in Genesis 1 is a kind of universal, priestly kingship — all humans, not just monarchs, are assigned royal governance of the creation.

צלם.bdb: image, likeness, idol — a visible representation or likeness of something; used of statues, idols, and the human image of God
צלם.explanation:
The noun *צלם (ṣelem)* means image, likeness, representation — originally a physical statue or idol. BDB: image — a representation of a person or deity in physical form. Strong's H6754 documents 17 occurrences, including the pivotal uses in Genesis 1:26–27.

In the ancient Near East, the *ṣelem* of a king was placed throughout his territory as a marker of his sovereign presence and authority — you could not see the king, but his image represented him. Genesis 1:26–27 applies this concept to humanity: humans are made as God's *ṣelem* — his representative images placed throughout creation to represent divine presence and exercise delegated governance. The theological argument is anti-idolatrous: if humans bear the divine image, then making an idol (a *ṣelem* of a deity) is a rival and illegitimate representation. Only the living human being is the legitimate *ṣelem* of God. This concept — the *imago Dei* — becomes the cornerstone of biblical anthropology and the foundation of human dignity across all subsequent theology.

כלה.gloss: to complete, to finish, to bring to an end
כלה.bdb: to be finished, to be completed; Piel: to complete, to finish; Hiphil: to end
כלה.explanation: The root *כלה (kālāh)* means to complete, to finish, to bring to an end. In Genesis 2:1–2, it appears in both Pual (passive: the heavens and earth were finished) and Piel (active: God finished his work) forms, creating a theological bracket: creation received its completion (Pual) and God actively completed it (Piel). The noun *כלה (kallāh)* — bride — is from a different root. The verb *כלה* appears in Jeremiah 44:27 (the sword shall finish them off) and Nehemiah 6:15 (the wall was finished). The Sabbath theology derives from this root: the creation is fully complete (*kālāh*), leaving the seventh day as pure rest rather than continued work.

שבת.gloss: to cease, to rest, to stop
שבת.bdb: to cease, to desist, to rest — primarily cessation of activity rather than physical recuperation
שבת.explanation: The root *שבת (šāḇaṯ)* means to cease, to stop, to rest from activity. BDB distinguishes it from *נוח (nûaḥ)* — to rest/find rest — noting *šāḇaṯ* emphasizes cessation of activity. The noun *שבת (šabbāṯ)* — Sabbath — derives from this root. God *šāḇaṯ* on the seventh day (Genesis 2:2–3) not because he was tired but because the work was done. The same root appears in Exodus 31:17 (God rested and was refreshed). Exodus 20:11 grounds the Sabbath commandment directly in God's rest on the seventh day of creation. The root gives us both the verb (to sabbath = to cease) and the institution (the Sabbath day of cessation).

שבע.bdb: seven — cardinal number; ordinal form shiv'i — seventh
שבע.explanation: The root *שבע (šeḇaʿ)* provides the cardinal number seven and the ordinal *שביעי (šĕḇîʿî)* — seventh. Seven is the most sacred number in the Hebrew Bible's numerical system. The priestly calendar is built on sevens: seven days, seven weeks (Shavuot), seven months for the holy days, seven years (sabbatical year), 7×7+1 (Jubilee). The menorah has seven branches. The book of Revelation carries this sevenfold structure throughout. The seventh day (*haššĕḇîʿî*) of Genesis 2:2–3 is the first sacred time in Scripture, establishing the seven-pattern that will structure all subsequent Hebrew religious time. There is also a possible wordplay between *šeḇaʿ (seven)* and *šāḇaʿ (to swear)* — covenants were ratified with seven-based oaths.

קדש.gloss: to be holy, to be set apart
קדש.bdb: Qal: to be sacred/holy; Piel: to consecrate, to sanctify, to set apart as holy; Niphal: to show oneself holy
קדש.explanation: The root *קדש (qāḏaš)* means primarily to be set apart, to be holy, to be consecrated. BDB notes the fundamental meaning is separation and consecration for a special purpose. The first occurrence (Genesis 2:3) applies *qāḏaš* to time — the seventh day — before it is applied to any space or person. Holiness is first temporal before it is spatial or personal. The root underlies *קדוש (qāḏôš)* — holy (the divine attribute repeatedly declared by the seraphim in Isaiah 6:3: Holy, holy, holy is YHWH of hosts); *קדושה (qĕḏūšāh)* — holiness; *מקדש (miqdāš)* — sanctuary, holy place; and *קדש-קדשים (qŏḏeš haqqŏḏāšîm)* — Holy of Holies. The concept of holiness as set-apart-ness is the organizing principle of the entire priestly tradition.

ילד.gloss: to bear, to beget, to give birth
ילד.bdb: Qal: to bear, to give birth; Hiphil: to beget; Niphal/Pual: to be born
ילד.explanation: The root *ילד (yālaḏ)* means to bear children, to give birth, to beget. The noun *תולדות (tôlĕḏôṯ)* — generations, account — derives from this root and becomes the structural formula of Genesis (10 occurrences). The noun *ילד (yeleḏ)* — child — is also from this root, as are *ילדה (yalḏāh)* — girl — and *מולדת (môleḏeṯ)* — birth, native land. Genealogical lists in the Hebrew Bible are built from the Hiphil of *yālaḏ*: X fathered (*hôlîḏ*) Y. The *tôlĕḏôṯ* formula organizes Genesis into 10 named sections, turning the book of Genesis into a book of generations — a narrative genealogy of the cosmos.

צמח.gloss: to sprout, to spring up, to grow
צמח.bdb: Qal: to sprout, to spring up; Hiphil: to cause to sprout, to make grow
צמח.explanation: The root *צמח (ṣāmaḥ)* means to sprout, to spring up, to grow out of the ground. It is used of vegetation, hair, and figuratively of the growth of seed-ideas and dynasties. In Genesis 2:5 (*yiṣmāḥ*, Qal — no plant had yet sprung up) and 2:9 (*wayyaṣmaḥ*, Hiphil — God caused to spring up), the same root describes both the natural-world's vegetation capacity and God's active cultivation of the garden. Most significantly, *ṣemaḥ (צמח)* — the Sprout/Branch — becomes a messianic title in the prophets: Isaiah 4:2 (the Branch of YHWH will be beautiful and glorious), Jeremiah 23:5 and 33:15 (righteous Branch for David), Zechariah 3:8 and 6:12 (my servant the Branch). Creation's sprouting and messianic salvation-history share the root.

מטר.bdb: Qal: to rain; Hiphil: to cause to rain, to send rain
מטר.explanation: The root *מטר (māṭar)* means to rain. The noun *מטר (māṭār)* — rain — is derived from it. In the Hebrew Bible, rain is consistently a divine act: YHWH sends or withholds rain according to the covenant relationship with Israel. The Hiphil (*himṭîr*, to cause rain) emphasizes God's agency. Key occurrences: Genesis 2:5 (had not yet caused it to rain), Genesis 7:4 (I will cause it to rain for 40 days), Genesis 19:24 (YHWH rained fire and sulfur on Sodom). Deuteronomy 11:14 promises rain as a covenant blessing; 11:17 threatens drought as a covenant curse. Elijah's pronouncement of drought (1 Kings 17:1) and the subsequent rain (18:1) are acts of prophetic covenant theology using this root.

נפח.bdb: to blow, to puff, to breathe into — of blowing wind, blowing on coals, and divine breath into humans
נפח.explanation: The root *נפח (nāp̄aḥ)* means to blow, to breathe with force. The Genesis 2:7 use (*wayyippāḥ bĕʾappāw nišmaṯ ḥayyîm* — he breathed into his nostrils the breath of life) is the most intimate of all creation acts. The same root appears in Ezekiel 37:9 (*pĕḥî*, blow!), where the divine wind is commanded to breathe into the dry bones — the valley vision that explicitly parallels the original creation of humanity in Genesis 2. Job 20:26 uses *nāp̄aḥ* of a fire devouring. The root is distinct from *rûaḥ* (wind/spirit) though both involve the idea of breath moving. The *nāp̄aḥ* of Genesis 2:7 is personal, directed, intimate — not the cosmic wind/spirit of Genesis 1:2 but the close breath of a parent animating a child.

נטע.bdb: to plant — of trees, vineyards, nations, and symbolically of divine acts
נטע.explanation: The root *נטע (nāṭaʿ)* means to plant, to set a plant in the ground. It is used for planting trees, vineyards, gardens, and metaphorically for establishing peoples in their land. In Genesis 2:8, God plants (*wayyiṭṭaʿ*) the garden of Eden. The same verb appears in Isaiah 51:16 (I have planted the heavens), Jeremiah 18:9 (building and planting nations), Psalm 44:2 (God planted our fathers), and Ezekiel 17 (the planting of the royal cedar, an allegory of the Davidic dynasty). The verb always implies intentional, deliberate establishment by an agent with purpose and commitment. God as gardener-planter is a foundational metaphor for divine providential care.

עדן.bdb: delight, luxury — the primordial garden; as a place name: Eden
עדן.explanation: The root *עדן (ʿāḏan)* means to experience delight, luxury, or soft pleasure. The place name *עדן (ʿêḏen)* — Eden — encodes this meaning: the primordial garden is the place of divine delight. The related noun *ʿeḏānîm* — luxuries, delights — appears in Nehemiah 9:25. Eden as a place name and concept appears throughout the prophets as the paradigm of restored paradise: Isaiah 51:3 (YHWH will make Zion like Eden), Ezekiel 28:13 (you were in Eden, the garden of God), Ezekiel 31:9 (no tree in Eden was its equal in beauty), Joel 2:3 (behind them a desolate wilderness, before them like the garden of Eden). The LXX translates Eden as *paradeisos* — paradise — giving Western languages their word for the original divine sanctuary.

שקה.bdb: Qal/Hiphil: to water, to irrigate, to give drink; related noun: šāqeh — cupbearer
שקה.explanation: The root *שקה (šāqāh)* means to water, to give drink, to irrigate. In Genesis 2:6 and 10, it describes the ground-water system that waters the garden before and after rain. The related noun *šāqeh (שקה)* — cupbearer — appears in the Joseph story: the chief cupbearer (*śar hammaš·qîm*) who serves Pharaoh (Genesis 40:1, 21; 41:9) has a title derived from this root. The priestly temple service and the garden watering share the same verbal root — both are acts of *šāqāh* serving a sacred space. The imagery of divine watering appears in Psalm 65:9–10 (God waters the earth, enriching it greatly) and Isaiah 58:11 (YHWH will guide you... and you shall be like a watered garden).

יצר.gloss: to form, to shape, to fashion
יצר.bdb: to form, to shape — specifically of a potter working clay; by extension of any deliberate shaping
יצר.explanation: The root *יצר (yāṣar)* means to form, to shape, to fashion — specifically the action of a potter. BDB notes the characteristic use for a craftsman working material (particularly clay) into a desired shape. The double *yôd* in the MT of Genesis 2:7 (*wayyîyyeṣer*, with two yods, unique in the text) has been interpreted rabbinically as pointing to the double formation: earthly body and heavenly soul. The root gives the noun *יצר (yêṣer)* — inclination, impulse — in the phrase *yêṣer hārāʿ* (evil inclination) and *yêṣer haṭṭôḇ* (good inclination) from rabbinic theology. In the prophets, God-as-potter is a persistent metaphor: Isaiah 29:16 (can the clay say to the potter?), Isaiah 45:9 (woe to the one who strives with his Maker), Isaiah 64:8 (we are the clay, you are our potter), Jeremiah 18 (the parable of the potter's house).

קדם.gloss: the east, what is in front, antiquity
קדם.bdb: what is in front; east (direction of sunrise); antiquity, what is before; Hiphil: to come before, to meet
קדם.explanation: The root *קדם (qāḏam)* and its derivatives cover two related meanings: spatial (east, the direction one faces) and temporal (what is before/ancient/primary). The noun *קדם (qeḏem)* appears in Genesis 2:8 (*miqqeḏem*, in the east) and 11:2 (moving east toward Shinar). East is the direction of sacred orientation in Hebrew cosmology: the tabernacle and temple face east, the glory of God enters from the east (Ezekiel 43:2), and the Messiah is associated with the rising sun. The temporal sense gives *miqqeḏem* the meaning of old, of the beginning — ancient times. Both senses converge in Eden: the east is where the primordial sanctuary lies, and it is the beginning/origin of all things. After the fall, humans are expelled eastward — eastward is simultaneously toward origin and toward exile.

עבד.gloss: to work, to serve, to till, to worship
עבד.bdb: to work, to labor, to serve — agricultural labor, priestly service, and worship are all covered by this root
עבד.explanation: The root *עבד (ʿāḇaḏ)* encompasses the full range from physical agricultural labor (to till the ground) to covenant service (to serve a master) to ritual worship (to serve God). BDB notes that all three senses are inseparably linked in the Hebrew worldview. The noun *עבד (ʿeḇeḏ)* — servant/slave — and *עבודה (ʿăḇôḏāh)* — service/worship/work — derive from this root. In Genesis 2:5, 15, working the ground (*laʿăḇōḏ ʾeṯ hāʾădāmāh*) is the first human vocation. The same vocabulary appears for Levitical service in the tabernacle (Numbers 3:7–8; 18:7). The human in the garden is priest-farmer, and farm-work is sanctuary-service. This dual meaning is crucial: human labor in the world is inherently priestly when understood through the Edenic template.

שמר.gloss: to keep, to guard, to observe, to watch
שמר.bdb: to keep, to guard, to watch over — military guarding, observance of commandments, and covenant faithfulness
שמר.explanation: The root *שמר (šāmar)* means to keep, to guard, to watch over, to observe. It covers: military guarding (keeping watch), ritual observance (keeping the Sabbath, keeping commandments), covenantal faithfulness (keeping the covenant), and relational care (keeping a person safe). In Genesis 2:15, the human is placed in the garden *lĕšāmĕrāh* — to keep/guard it. The same verb pair (*ʿāḇaḏ + šāmar*) is used for Levitical duties in Numbers 3:7–8 and 18:7. The Aaronic blessing uses *šāmar*: YHWH bless you and keep you (*yišmĕreḵā*, Numbers 6:24). The Shema mentions *šāmar* in requiring observance: keep (*šāmar*) all his commandments (Deuteronomy 26:17). Israel as keeper of the covenant is the national expansion of the human's garden vocation of keeping the sanctuary.

צוה.gloss: to command, to charge, to commission
צוה.bdb: Piel: to command, to charge, to give instructions, to appoint
צוה.explanation: The root *צוה (ṣāwāh)* means to command, to give instructions, to charge — always in the Piel stem. BDB notes it often implies a commissioning relationship: the commander has the authority and the commissioned one has the obligation. The noun *מצוה (miṣwāh)* — commandment — derives from this root and becomes the defining word of Torah-obligation: the 613 *miṣwôṯ* of rabbinic tradition. Genesis 2:16 is the first divine command to a human being. The Pentateuch's legal corpus (Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy) uses this verb hundreds of times as YHWH commissions Moses with specific instructions. The garden command is the proto-Torah: like the full Torah, it combines unrestricted generosity with defined limits.

חמד.gloss: to desire, to covet, to delight in
חמד.bdb: to desire, to covet, to take pleasure in — morally neutral; context determines whether the desire is legitimate or forbidden
חמד.explanation: The root *חמד (ḥāmaḏ)* means to desire, to covet, to find desirable. The root is morally neutral — the goodness of the desire depends entirely on its object. In Genesis 2:9, the trees are *neḥmāḏ* (Niphal: desirable) — this is appropriate, God-given aesthetic desire. In Genesis 3:6, the woman sees the tree is *neḥmāḏ* (the same Niphal) — the same looking becomes the temptation. The Tenth Commandment uses the Qal: do not *tāḥmōḏ* — do not covet — your neighbor's house, wife, field (Exodus 20:17; Deuteronomy 5:21). In Psalm 68:16, God *ḥāmaḏ* the mountain where he would dwell — God covets/desires Zion. The noun *ḥemḏāh* — precious thing, desirable object — appears throughout the Psalms and wisdom literature.

ידע.gloss: to know, to understand, to experience intimately
ידע.bdb: to know — intellectual, experiential, and relational/intimate knowing; all three are covered by the single root
ידע.explanation: The root *ידע (yāḏaʿ)* means to know in the fullest sense — intellectual knowledge, experiential knowledge, relational intimacy, and sexual union are all expressed by this one root. BDB covers these senses comprehensively. The noun *דעת (daʿaṯ)* — knowledge — derives from this root. In Genesis 2:9, 17, the tree of knowledge (*ʿêṣ hadda·ʿaṯ ṭôḇ wārāʿ*) offers comprehensive experiential moral knowledge. In Genesis 4:1, Adam *knew (yāḏaʿ)* his wife — sexual intimacy. In Exodus 6:3, God notes the patriarchs did not *know (yāḏaʿ)* him by his name YHWH — covenant-personal knowledge. In Amos 3:2, YHWH *yāḏaʿ* Israel alone of all the nations — covenant election. Knowledge, in Hebrew, is always personal, relational, and involving. Pure propositional knowledge without relationship is a deficient concept in the Hebrew worldview.

פרד.gloss: to divide, to separate, to part
פרד.bdb: Niphal: to be divided, to separate; Hiphil: to divide, to cause separation; Piel: to scatter
פרד.explanation: The root *פרד (pāraḏ)* means to divide, to separate, to part ways. In Genesis 2:10, the Eden-river divides (*yippārêḏ*, Niphal) into four streams. In Genesis 13:9, 11, Abraham and Lot separate (*niprĕḏāh*, Niphal — let us separate). In Ruth 1:17, Ruth vows that only death will separate (*yap·rîḏ*, Hiphil) her from Naomi. Each separation in Genesis marks a progressive distancing from the divine center: Eden divides into four, Abraham/Lot separate when the land cannot hold them together, and ultimately the human couple is separated from the garden. Division and separation are theologically fraught movements in Genesis — they either describe the abundance that requires spreading out or the ruptures that result from sin.

נהר.bdb: river — the major watercourse in a region; metaphorically used for the river of Paradise and the eschatological river from the temple
נהר.explanation: The noun *נהר (nāhār)* means river — a flowing body of water. In Genesis 2:10, the Eden-river (*nāhār*) flows from the divine garden outward to the whole world. The same word is used for the Nile (Exodus 1:22), the Euphrates (*hannahār haggāḏôl*, the great river), and the heavenly river in prophetic vision. Isaiah 33:21 promises YHWH as a place of rivers (*nĕhārîm*) for the restored Jerusalem. Ezekiel 47:1–12 describes *mayim yōṣĕʾîm* — waters going out — from the temple threshold (using *yāṣāʾ*, the same root as Genesis 2:10), which heal the salt sea and bring life wherever they flow. Revelation 22:1 shows *ποταμὸν ὕδατος ζωῆς* — the river of the water of life — flowing from the throne. The theological arc: Genesis 2's Eden-river → Ezekiel 47's temple-river → Revelation 22's throne-river.

לקח.gloss: to take, to receive, to seize
לקח.bdb: to take, to take hold of, to receive, to seize — with a wide range from grasping to receiving a gift to marriage
לקח.explanation: The root *לקח (lāqaḥ)* means to take, to receive. It appears three times in Genesis 2 with God as subject: taking the human (placing him in the garden, v.15), taking a rib (building the woman, v.21), and taking from the man (v.22). The verb covers all kinds of taking: seizing, receiving as a gift, and taking in marriage (also a primary meaning — *lāqaḥ ʾiššāh* means to take a wife). The same root provides the word *miqdāš* in Exodus 25:8 (take for me an offering). In a reflexive sense, *lāqaḥ* also appears in wisdom texts: wisdom *takes* the heart, instruction *takes* hold of the mind. The breadth of the root — from physical grasping to marital taking to receiving divine instruction — makes it one of the most semantically rich verbs in Biblical Hebrew.

נוח.gloss: to rest, to settle, to find repose
נוח.bdb: to rest, to settle down, to be at ease; Hiphil: to cause to rest, to set down, to give rest
נוח.explanation: The root *נוח (nûaḥ)* means to rest, to settle, to find repose. The name Noah (*nûaḥ*) is etymologically connected to this root (Genesis 5:29: this one will comfort us from our work and the toil of our hands). God settles the human in the garden (Hiphil: *wayyannîḥêhū*, v.15) — the same root as Sabbath-rest theology and Noah's comforting role. The Hiphil *hinnîaḥ* — to cause to rest/settle — appears in Deuteronomy 12:10 (God gives rest from enemies in the promised land) and 2 Samuel 7:11 (I will give you rest from all your enemies — the Davidic covenant promise). The theological arc: God rests on the seventh day, God settles the human in the garden, God will give Israel rest in the land, and ultimately God will give the final eschatological rest to his people (Isaiah 14:3; Micah 2:10).

בנה.bdb: to build, to construct — of physical structures but also of families and dynasties
בנה.explanation: The root *בנה (bānāh)* means to build, to construct. In Genesis 2:22 it is applied to the creation of the woman: God built (*wayyiḇen*) the rib into a woman. The same root is used for building cities (Genesis 4:17 — Cain built a city), building the tower of Babel (Genesis 11:4–5), building altars (Genesis 8:20; 12:7–8), building the tabernacle (Exodus 40:18), building the temple (1 Kings 6:14). Figuratively, *bānāh* is used of establishing a family: God will build (*yiḇneh*) you a house (2 Samuel 7:27 — the Davidic covenant). The woman built from the rib is the first building, the garden's first architectural resident. The noun *בניין (binyān)* — building — and the name *Benjamin (binyāmîn)* — son of the right hand — derive from the same root.

צלע.bdb: rib (of the body); side (of a structure); side-chamber (of the tabernacle or temple)
צלע.explanation: The noun *צלע (ṣêlāʿ)* means rib (anatomical) and side or lateral chamber (architectural). The same word describes the side-planks of the tabernacle (Exodus 26:20–27), the side-chambers of the temple (1 Kings 6:5–8), and the rib of the human body from which the woman was built. This is not a coincidence in the text's usage: the woman built from the man's *ṣêlāʿ* and the tabernacle/temple built from *ṣĕlāʿôṯ* share the same constructional vocabulary. The woman as living sanctuary and the sanctuary as feminine architectural space are linked by the word. Both are divine constructions from *ṣêlāʿ* material. The word also appears in 2 Samuel 16:13 (Shimei threw stones from the hillside — *ṣelaʿ*, a side/slope) and in architectural descriptions throughout Ezekiel's temple vision.

סגר.gloss: to close, to shut, to seal
סגר.bdb: to close, to shut — of doors, gates, wounds, and sealed spaces
סגר.explanation: The root *סגר (sāḡar)* means to close, to shut, to seal. In Genesis 2:21, God closes (*wayyisḡōr*) the flesh of the man's side after removing the rib. In Genesis 7:16, YHWH shut (*wayyisḡōr*) the door of Noah's ark. Both closings are divine acts that seal and protect life: the sealed flesh protects the man, the sealed ark protects Noah. The same verb appears in Leviticus (of the priest shutting/isolating a person with suspected skin disease for examination), in Joshua 6:1 (Jericho was shut up), and in Isaiah 45:1 (God opens doors before Cyrus that are never shut). *Sāḡar* is the opposite of *pāṯaḥ* (to open): God opens and closes, shuts and unseals, as sovereign acts of providential care.

דבק.gloss: to cling, to cleave, to hold fast
דבק.bdb: to cling, to stick, to hold fast — of physical adhesion, personal loyalty, and covenantal attachment
דבק.explanation: The root *דבק (dāḇaq)* means to cling, to cleave, to stick to. It is used for: physical adhesion (Job 29:10 — the tongue clings to the roof of the mouth), marital union (Genesis 2:24 — he shall cleave to his wife), national loyalty (Ruth 1:14 — Ruth clung to Naomi), and covenantal attachment to God (Deuteronomy 10:20; 11:22; 13:4; 30:20 — cleave to YHWH your God; Joshua 23:8). The noun *dʾḇeqeṯ* — joint, solder, adhesive — derives from the root. The theological significance: the same word used for marital cleaving and theological cleaving to YHWH makes marriage and covenant fidelity structurally identical at the verbal level. This is the basis for the prophetic use of marriage as a metaphor for the YHWH-Israel relationship (Hosea, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Isaiah 54, 62).

עזב.gloss: to leave, to forsake, to abandon
עזב.bdb: to leave, to forsake — can be neutral departure or sinful abandonment depending on context
עזב.explanation: The root *עזב (ʿāzaḇ)* means to leave, to forsake, to abandon. In Genesis 2:24, it describes the son's legitimate departure from his parents to establish a new household — necessary and proper separation. In the prophets, the same verb describes Israel's sinful forsaking of YHWH: Jeremiah 2:13 (they have forsaken me, the fountain of living water), Isaiah 1:4 (they have forsaken YHWH), Isaiah 42:16 (I will not forsake them). The covenant consequence of forsaking YHWH is divine abandonment in return (Ezekiel 8:12; 9:9). The word creates a theological irony: the proper leaving (*ʿāzaḇ*) of parents to cleave to a spouse is used as the metaphor for Israel's improper leaving (*ʿāzaḇ*) of YHWH to chase other gods. Marriage-leaving and God-forsaking share a root.

עצם.bdb: bone — of the skeleton; body; self; essence, substance
עצם.explanation: The noun *עצם (ʿeṣem)* means bone — the hard structural element of the body. By extension it means the body itself, one's essential self, or the very substance of something. BDB records: bone, body (of the body as identified with the essential self), the very day itself (*ʿeṣem hayyôm hazzeh* — on this very day). The kinship formula in Genesis 2:23 (*ʿeṣem mêʾăṣāmay* — bone of my bones) declares maximum possible kinship. This formula appears in: Genesis 29:14 (Laban to Jacob: you are my bone and flesh), 2 Samuel 5:1 (tribes to David: we are your bone and flesh), 2 Samuel 19:13 (David to Amasa), Judges 9:2. The formula is always a declaration of primary loyalty and kinship obligation — to share bones is to share everything.

בוש.gloss: to be ashamed, to be put to shame
בוש.bdb: Qal: to be ashamed; Hiphil: to shame; Hitpael: to feel shame within oneself, to be put to shame
בוש.explanation: The root *בוש (bûš)* means to be ashamed, to experience confusion and disgrace. BDB distinguishes between external shame (public disgrace) and internal shame (experiential confusion). The Hitpael (*yiṯbōšāšū*, Genesis 2:25) is the intensive reflexive — they did not feel shame within themselves. Shame (*bôšāh, bûššāh*) appears as a major theological category in the prophets, particularly in covenantal breach: Israel will be ashamed when they realize their idols could not save (Isaiah 1:29; 44:9–11; Jeremiah 2:26; 10:14). The promise of eschatological restoration is often framed as the removal of shame: *you will not be ashamed or humiliated* (Isaiah 54:4; Zephaniah 3:11). The shame of Genesis 3:7 (after the fall) and the shamelessness of Genesis 2:25 (before the fall) mark the theological boundary of the fall narrative.

פעם.bdb: foot, step; occurrence, time (as in 'this time'); pulse, beat
פעם.explanation: The noun *פעם (paʿam)* means foot, step, or beat/occurrence. As a temporal expression, *hap·pa·ʿam* — this time/this occasion — marks a decisive moment: Genesis 2:23 (at last! this one!), Genesis 29:35 (this time I will praise YHWH — Leah at Judah's birth), Genesis 30:20 (this time my husband will honor me — Leah at Zebulun's birth), Genesis 46:30 (Jacob: now let me die, since I have seen your face). The word covers both rhythmic physical motion (foot-beats, steps) and the occurrence-count of temporal events. The related word *פעמיים (paʿamayim)* — twice (two feet) — shows the connection between the foot/step root and the temporal counting usage.

ערם.bdb: naked, bare — without clothing; the adjective of physical nakedness
ערם.explanation: The adjective *ערם (ʿārôm)* means naked, bare, unclothed. In Genesis 2:25, *ʿărûmmîm* (plural: both of them naked) describes the innocence of Adam and Eve before the fall. In Genesis 3:7, after eating, *wayyêḏĕʿû kî ʿêrûmmîm hēm* — they knew they were naked. The consonants *ʿ-r-m* appear in Genesis 3:1 with different vowels as *ʿārûm* (crafty, shrewd) describing the serpent — a deliberate homophonic wordplay connecting the naked-innocent of 2:25 with the shrewd-tempter of 3:1. The same consonants, two radically different realities. The shame of nakedness becomes, in the prophets, a metaphor for the exposure of sin: Ezekiel 16:37 (God will expose Jerusalem's nakedness before her lovers), Hosea 2:3 (strip her naked). Covering nakedness becomes a metaphor for covenant protection: Ezekiel 16:8 (I spread my cloak over you).
